title: Louie Bumps Into An Old Lady

jokes.text: Louie's still coming on to the new cab driver, huh? Yeah. I don't know how any woman can resist him, you know?
jokes.setup: Louie is trying to seduce Janine with his 'mentoring' offer
jokes.punchline: The obvious sarcasm about Louie's irresistible appeal
jokes.explanation: Standard workplace commentary on Louie's delusions, well-executed but predictable

jokes.text: Score. Score.
jokes.setup: Louie offers to take Janine out for 'training'
jokes.punchline: The guys immediately recognize his transparent pickup attempt
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the repetition and timing make it land well

jokes.text: Look, Janine, don't... don't listen to them. That's just a little of their crude garage humor.
jokes.setup: Louie was just caught trying to pick up Janine
jokes.punchline: He tries to position himself as the gentleman while being the worst offender
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - his complete lack of self-awareness is funny and character-specific

jokes.text: Banta, I was driving cabs when you were in the third grade... Your first year in third grade.
jokes.setup: Louie brags about his experience driving cabs
jokes.punchline: The implication that Tony failed third grade initially
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult with perfect timing and structure - the delayed punchline makes it memorable

jokes.text: I told her, 'If he gives you any trouble just rap him on the snout with a rolled-up newspaper.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about how to handle Louie's advances
jokes.punchline: Treating Louie like a misbehaving dog
jokes.explanation: Vivid imagery that perfectly captures Louie's behavior - original and visual

jokes.text: The way he treats women like objects, pieces of meat. I mean, doesn't he know that thinking went out two to three months ago?
jokes.setup: Criticism of Louie's outdated attitude toward women
jokes.punchline: The absurdly recent timeframe for when sexism 'went out of style'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly subverts expectations - turns what seems like feminist commentary into period-specific humor about recent social changes

jokes.text: That's because you don't have a great pair of legs. Yeah, but Louie don't know that.
jokes.setup: Tony explains why Louie won't teach Jim to drive
jokes.punchline: Jim's logic that Louie can't see his legs anyway
jokes.explanation: Jim's innocent confusion about cause and effect is perfectly in character

jokes.text: Have you ever noticed... that when you put the pencil behind your ear it tends to slither out when you go around sharp curves?
jokes.setup: Jim has a driving tip for Janine
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and useless observation about pencils 'slithering'
jokes.explanation: Pure Jim Ignatowski - the word 'slither' for a pencil falling is perfect character writing

jokes.text: Voila.
jokes.setup: Jim promises a solution to the pencil problem
jokes.punchline: His 'solution' is presumably ridiculous (staplers the pencil to his head or similar)
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the visual reveal we can't see, but Jim's confident presentation is funny

jokes.text: Well, what difference does it make as long as she's okay and nobody got pictures?
jokes.setup: Louie is confronted about running over an old lady
jokes.punchline: His main concern is avoiding photographic evidence
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's amoral pragmatism - dark but character-appropriate

jokes.text: Oh, poor thing. Hasn't she suffered enough?
jokes.setup: Discussion of Louie spending time with the injured woman
jokes.punchline: The implication that time with Louie is additional suffering
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical joke that builds on established character dynamics

jokes.text: He's trying to make friends with her so she won't sue him for his eyeballs.
jokes.setup: Speculation about Louie's motives for being nice
jokes.punchline: The colorful expression 'sue him for his eyeballs'
jokes.explanation: Solid but not exceptional - the colorful language elevates a basic observation

jokes.text: where 'safety first' is our only motto.
jokes.setup: Louie introduces the Sunshine Cab Company
jokes.punchline: The ironic safety motto from someone who just ran over a customer
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony that plays well given the recent accident

jokes.text: She'll be up and doddering about any time now.
jokes.setup: Louie reassures everyone about Edith's recovery
jokes.punchline: The word 'doddering' which emphasizes her age rather than health
jokes.explanation: Louie's insensitive word choice reveals his true feelings despite his fake concern

jokes.text: We wouldn't want to have any accidents.
jokes.setup: Louie is giving Edith a slow tour of the garage
jokes.punchline: The ironic concern about accidents from the person who caused her injury
jokes.explanation: Predictable irony but serviceable

jokes.text: Well, thanks for picking up the conversation, Rieger.
jokes.setup: Alex asks Edith if they've met, she gives a generic grandmother response
jokes.punchline: Louie's sarcastic thanks for Alex's conversation-killing question
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Louie's frustration at Alex interrupting his charm offensive

jokes.text: Do you know how many jelly beans I can hold in my mouth at one time?
jokes.setup: Louie is trying to impress Edith with the quality of his drivers
jokes.punchline: Jim's completely random and inappropriate question
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - completely derails Louie's presentation with innocent randomness

jokes.text: Well, I'll have to find out somewhere else.
jokes.setup: Edith says she doesn't know about the jelly beans
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact response like this is important research
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery makes Jim's absurd priorities even funnier

jokes.text: And as you can see, we also hire those less fortunate than ourselves.
jokes.setup: Jim has just embarrassed Louie in front of Edith
jokes.punchline: Louie's condescending dismissal of Jim as 'less fortunate'
jokes.explanation: Good recovery by Louie, maintains his character while addressing the situation

jokes.text: No. This is her nap time.
jokes.setup: Edith asks if Louie's mother passed away when he mentions 'God rest her soul'
jokes.punchline: The revelation that his mother is just napping
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect timing - subverts the emotional moment brilliantly

jokes.text: Yes. My lawyers are forcing me into suing you for a million dollars.
jokes.setup: Edith has been praising Louie and their friendship
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of suing him for a million dollars
jokes.explanation: Great plot twist delivered with perfect casualness - the contrast makes it hilarious

jokes.text: Everything, including the gas I used when I ran her over.
jokes.setup: Louie explains he has to pay for everything
jokes.punchline: The absurdly detailed accounting including gas costs
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that shows the absurd bureaucracy of the situation

jokes.text: Louie's math: '$100 a week... a million bucks would take... two... 200 years'
jokes.setup: Louie trying to calculate how long it would take to pay off the debt
jokes.punchline: His visible struggle with basic math and horrifying realization
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Louie's mental limitations under stress

jokes.text: Yeah, well... that sounds a lot longer than it really is.
jokes.setup: Louie calculates it will take 200 years to pay off the debt
jokes.punchline: Tony's attempt to be encouraging with completely illogical reassurance
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd logic delivered with complete sincerity - the math makes no sense but the delivery sells it

jokes.text: I know that feeling.
jokes.setup: Louie says Edith will wish she never heard the name Louie De Palma
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry acknowledgment that everyone feels that way
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alex response - economical and character-appropriate

jokes.text: If I can't beat your rap, I'll eat a bug.
jokes.setup: Louie is looking for cheap legal representation
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous guarantee from a bottom-tier lawyer
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor that establishes the lawyer's quality level

jokes.text: Poor lady's got rough luck.
jokes.setup: Alex explains that Edith has been 'hit' by cabs monthly for years
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic assessment of her 'luck'
jokes.explanation: Clean delivery of the obvious irony - functional but not exceptional

jokes.text: I knew it wasn't my fault!
jokes.setup: Learning that Edith is a con artist
jokes.punchline: Louie's immediate shift to claiming innocence despite actually hitting her
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie logic - he did hit her but now feels vindicated

jokes.text: Besides, with a nose like that you're better than a bloodhound.
jokes.setup: Louie is trying to convince Alex to help find Bergmeyer
jokes.punchline: The insult disguised as a compliment about Alex's tracking abilities
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - insults people while asking for favors

jokes.text: That's what you consider sweet-talking, Louie?
jokes.setup: Louie just insulted Alex's nose while asking for help
jokes.punchline: Alex's incredulous response to Louie's 'charm'
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response that highlights Louie's social ineptitude

jokes.text: 'That's what you consider sweet-talking, Louie?'
jokes.setup: Louie's insulting approach to asking for help
jokes.punchline: Alex calling out his terrible persuasion technique
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response highlighting Louie's social incompetence

jokes.text: Really? Did they get you on drugs? No. I was already on drugs.
jokes.setup: Bobby assumes Jim went to court for drug charges
jokes.punchline: Jim's matter-of-fact correction about his drug state
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim logic - completely literal and innocent while being accidentally funny

jokes.text: Yeah. They got to be legal though, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Bobby suggests picking up 'legal secretaries'
jokes.punchline: Tony's misunderstanding about age of consent vs. profession
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that works but isn't particularly sophisticated

jokes.text: Come on, Edith, save it for the judge.
jokes.setup: Edith claims her injury is real this time
jokes.punchline: Tom's dismissive response treating it as another con
jokes.explanation: Functional but predictable - sets up the irony that she's actually hurt

jokes.text: Well, there's a first time for everything.
jokes.setup: Learning that Edith is actually injured for once
jokes.punchline: Tom's dry acknowledgment of the rare genuine injury
jokes.explanation: Serviceable deadpan response to the ironic situation

jokes.text: What's what?
jokes.setup: Jim says he has something important to tell Louie before he forgets
jokes.punchline: He immediately forgets what he was going to say
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - the setup and payoff are instant and character-perfect

jokes.text: He jumped out of the cab.
jokes.setup: Alex explains Bergmeyer's reaction to learning it's Louie he'd be helping
jokes.punchline: The immediate physical rejection of helping Louie
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy that shows how universally disliked Louie is

jokes.text: The system works.
jokes.setup: Alex tells Louie what Bergmeyer said about the old lady taking Louie's money
jokes.punchline: Bergmeyer's satisfaction that justice is being served to Louie
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical line that captures the universal sentiment about Louie getting his comeuppance

jokes.text: Our deal's off, Aardvark nose.
jokes.setup: Louie's plan has failed and Alex mentioned their deal
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately breaks his promise and insults Alex's nose again
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - can't help himself from being petty even when desperate

jokes.text: I smell blood.
jokes.setup: Louie decides to cross-examine Edith himself
jokes.punchline: His overconfident assessment of his legal abilities
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Louie's impending disaster - his arrogance is character-appropriate

jokes.text: Don't worry. She'll get out. She'll get out of the chair. She'll get out before she hits the stairs. Don't worry. Jump out, Edith. Jump out, Edith!
jokes.setup: Louie pushes Edith's wheelchair toward the stairs to prove she's faking
jokes.punchline: His panicked realization she's not jumping out as the wheelchair gains speed
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating panic as Louie's plan goes horribly wrong - the repetition builds perfectly

jokes.text: Rieger, what floor are we on? The 16th.
jokes.setup: Louie has just sent an elderly woman in a wheelchair down the courthouse stairs
jokes.punchline: The revelation of just how far she fell
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy timing - the height makes the situation exponentially worse

jokes.text: I have no further questions, Your Honor.
jokes.setup: Louie has just committed what appears to be attempted murder in court
jokes.punchline: His formal courtroom conclusion as if this was planned legal strategy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery after chaos - the formality makes it hilarious and perfectly captures Louie's delusion

jokes.text: Yeah, I'll say. I never seen a judge try to hit anybody with his gavel before.
jokes.setup: Discussion about how mad the judge was
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the judge tried to physically assault Louie
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the courtroom chaos - absurd but believable given what Louie did

tmdb_overview: Louie, driving a cab in order to give pointers to a new driver, accidentally runs into an old lady pedestrian.  Louie starts catering to the old lady, in the hopes of avoiding being sued, but soon learns she is still going to sue him for a million dollars.  Alex notices that the old lady looks familiar, and recognizes her as a con artist, who pulls scams like this on cab drivers all the time (faking injuries in order to sue them).  Alex makes a deal with Louie to find some of her previous victims to testify against her, if Louie agrees to treat Alex with more respect.  It turns out, however, that this time, the woman really has broken her leg this time, and is indeed confined to a wheelchair legitimately (she tells this to her lawyer without any of the gang around).  At the trial, Alex shows up alone because he couldn't get anyone to testify in Louie's behalf, so Louie gets a bright idea.  In an attempt to prove that the woman is faking an injury, in front of the court, Louie pushes he
insight: Louie's mundane collision spawns 44 jokes at season's slowest pace, emphasizing character over punchline.
